summary: We lived about 10 miles from New York City, and from the top floor of my high school, you could see all the buildings there. It was tough to focus on school when you could see the city and wanted to be there. I used to go into New York to visit theaters like the Roxy and Capitol, and if you had the money, the Paramount was the ultimate place to go.

I often went to the Roxy Theatre in New York City during the 1930s to enjoy entertainment. It was one of the places where you could see movies and big bands perform. Going there was part of my early exposure to the swing era and big band music.

The Capitol Theatre in New York City was another favorite spot of mine in the 1930s. Like the Roxy, it was a place to see movies and big band performances. I was lucky to attend these shows, often accompanied by my brother, which deepened my love for swing music.

The Paramount Theatre was the ultimate place to go if you had the money back in the 1930s. It combined movies with big band performances, making it a special experience. I remember seeing bands like Gene Krupa and Benny Goodman there, which was a big deal for me as a young fan of swing music.

Gene Krupa was my favorite bandleader during the swing era. I saw his band perform at places like the Paramount Theatre in New York City. His music was a big part of the dances and parties I attended with my siblings and their friends in the 1930s.

I had the chance to see Benny Goodman perform live at theaters like the Roxy and Capitol in New York City during the 1930s. He was one of the great bandleaders of the swing era, and his music was a big influence on me. My brother often took me along to these shows, which was a real treat.

Tommy Dorsey was one of the big band leaders whose performances I enjoyed in New York City theaters during the 1930s. Along with his brother Jimmy Dorsey, they were part of the great bands I saw as a young person. These experiences helped shape my love for swing music and dancing.

Jimmy Dorsey, like his brother Tommy, was a prominent bandleader I saw perform in New York City during the 1930s. Their music was part of the big band scene that I grew up loving. My brother often brought me to these shows, which were memorable moments from my youth.

I clearly remember the morning of December 7th, 1941, when the Japanese bombed Pearl Harbor. We were having breakfast, and my brother, who worked at a grocery store, ran home shouting the news. At the time, most of us didn’t fully understand what it meant, but it marked the beginning of a very difficult period for our family and the country.

My grandfather often talked about what was happening in Europe during the war years. We were very aware of the events overseas, especially as the war escalated. My brother eventually went to fight in Europe, and we followed the news closely, worried about all the boys who were drafted.

I grew up in Paterson, New Jersey, where my father owned a grocery store. During the war, we would travel around Paterson raising money for war bonds using a horse and wagon to save gasoline. Paterson was also home to my high school, which was featured in the movie Lean on Me, although it was a very different place when I attended.

My father’s older sibling lived in Nyack, New York, which was a mostly non-Jewish community at the time. His family was the only Jewish family there, and they had a gas station on Route 9. We often went there on Sundays during the war to bring them food, as my grandmother worried they might be starving without access to Jewish food.

Route 9 in New York was where my uncle’s family had a gas station in Nyack. We traveled there frequently during the war to bring food, especially since they were the only Jewish family in that area. The route also took us through Sleepy Hollow, where my dad sometimes drove very slowly after having a drink or two.

On our trips back from Nyack, we would drive through Sleepy Hollow, New York. I remember my dad sometimes had a drink or two more than he should have, so we would go about three miles an hour through that area. It was a small but memorable part of our family outings during the war.

My brother and my uncle’s two boys were all drafted into the United States Army during World War II. My brother was eager to join despite some physical challenges and eventually served in the Army, where he was a replacement during the Battle of the Bulge. The Army was a big part of our family’s experience during the war, and we anxiously followed the news about their safety.

I remember listening to President Franklin D. Roosevelt on the radio during the war years. He was a reassuring voice for many of us. I was born under Herbert Hoover, but it wasn’t until Roosevelt became president that things really started to improve, especially with food availability during the Depression and war.

I was born during Herbert Hoover’s presidency, which was a tough time for many families. People often joked about how things improved only after Roosevelt took office. Hoover’s era was marked by hardship, and I remember it as a time when food and resources were scarce before the New Deal and wartime efforts helped.

I attended Paterson High School in New Jersey, the same school featured in the movie Lean on Me, although it was very different when I was there. The principal wore a celluloid collar, and the environment was more reserved. I wasn’t much of an athlete, but I was tall and the basketball coach wanted me to play center, though I declined after one tryout.

I graduated from high school around the beginning of the Korean War. This period was another time of military conflict following World War II, and it marked a transition in my life from school to adulthood. The war was part of the broader context of military service that affected many families like mine.

World War II deeply affected my family and community. My brother served in the Army and was killed in the Battle of the Bulge in 1945. During the war, my father raised money for war bonds, and we all lived with rationing and uncertainty. The war shaped much of my early adult life and family history.

My brother was killed in the Battle of the Bulge on January 15, 1945. He was a replacement soldier who had only been in the Army for six months. The battle was brutal, with freezing conditions and many soldiers suffering from frostbite. I have letters from him the day before he died, reassuring us he was okay, which makes the loss even more poignant.

My brother trained at Fort Dix in New Jersey before being sent overseas during World War II. At Fort Dix, they gave out sharpshooter medals, though my brother couldn’t shoot well since we never had guns at home. Fort Dix was the starting point of his military journey that ended tragically in Europe.

My brother was killed in Belgium during the Battle of the Bulge, one of the harshest battles of World War II. Although he was not buried in the largest cemetery there, he was the first Jewish soldier to be brought back home. Belgium is forever tied to our family’s sacrifice and loss during the war.

I learned Yiddish from my grandmother, who only spoke that language at home. She insisted I play cards only in Yiddish, which was a way to keep the language alive in our family. Yiddish was part of my cultural heritage and a connection to my grandmother during difficult times.
